summary:
The American Revolution (1765–1783 AD) was the political and military struggle through which thirteen British North American colonies declared independence and established the United States of America — the first modern democratic republic, whose founding documents articulated Enlightenment principles of natural rights, popular sovereignty, and limited government that would inspire revolutionary movements worldwide for two centuries. The Revolution's causes were rooted in the post–[[event:2332|Seven Years' War]] imperial reorganization: Britain, seeking to recover war debts, imposed new taxes (the [[event:2394|Stamp Act]], Townshend Acts) on the colonies without representation in Parliament, provoking the colonial argument 'no taxation without representation.' Escalating confrontations — the Boston Massacre (1770), the [[event:2351|Boston Tea Party]] (1773), the Coercive Acts — drove the colonies toward open rebellion, which began at Lexington and Concord in April 1775.

The [[event:2350|Declaration of Independence]] (July 4, 1776), primarily drafted by Thomas Jefferson, articulated the philosophical foundations of the new nation in Lockean terms: that all men are created equal, endowed with inalienable rights to life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness, and that government derives its just powers from the consent of the governed. The military war, in which George Washington's Continental Army faced the world's preeminent military power, was decided by French intervention after the American victory at [[event:2395|Saratoga]] (1777) and concluded at [[event:2352|Yorktown]] (1781). The [[event:2477|Treaty of Paris]] (1783) recognized American independence. The Revolution's constitutional legacy — the [[event:2396|Constitution]] (1787), the Federalist Papers, the Bill of Rights — created a republican framework that balanced popular democracy against institutional checks and balances, influencing the [[event:107|French Revolution]] and virtually every subsequent democratic constitution.
